preFix.title: Using compost to improve clay soil structure
preFix.subtitle: Building up organic matter in heavy clay to improve drainage, aeration, and workability
preFix.excerpt: Clay soil contains over 40 percent clay particles by weight. It retains nutrients well but drains poorly and becomes waterlogged in winter and brick-hard in summer. Adding 50 to 100 mm of mature compost per year, forked to 200 mm depth, raises the organic matter content and stimulates earthworm populations that physically open the clay structure. Measurable improvement in drainage and workability typically takes two to three years of consistent amendment.
preFix.sourceNotes: RHS guidance on clay soil management 2024. DEFRA Countryside Survey for UK clay soil distribution. Cardiff University soil science research on organic matter and aggregate stability. Soil Association guidance on building soil organic matter.

content.text: The common advice for clay soil is to add grit or sand. In practice, you need around 25 percent grit by soil volume to make a real difference. That is far more than any garden-scale job delivers. Compost works by a different route. It feeds earthworms and microbes that make
humus
, which binds clay into stable crumbs. The change takes years but is visible within the first season in earthworm numbers and how easily the soil works.

attrs.title: Why grit alone does not work

content.text: Adding coarse sand or
grit
to clay in typical garden quantities (a few buckets per square metre) fills the existing clay pores with fine grit and can make the drainage worse, not better.
Organic matter
works at much lower concentrations because it acts through chemistry and biology rather than physical displacement.
What you need

Mature finished compost, ideally 12 months old or more. Fresh or partially finished compost can temporarily tie up nitrogen as it breaks down. Allow 100 litres of compost per square metre for the first amendment, reducing to 50 litres in subsequent years.
Coarse woody mulch: wood chip, leaf mould, or straw. Spread on the surface between amendments to cut rain compaction and feed surface earthworms.
Working clay soil when it is waterlogged destroys the
aggregate structure

that takes years to build. Digging wet clay smears and compacts the particles, sealing pores and making drainage worse. Wait until the soil passes the ribbon test: when you roll a small ball between palms it does not stick or leave wet marks on your hand before working it.
Autumn amendment (year one)
Spread a 100 mm layer of mature compost over the entire planting area in autumn before the ground freezes. Do not dig it in yet: allow earthworms and frost to begin incorporating it over winter.

In early spring when soil is workable, fork the compost layer into the top 200 mm using a garden fork. Work in rows, pushing the fork in vertically and levering back without inverting the soil. Inversion buries the surface biology and brings sub-soil to the top.

Apply a 50 mm layer of wood chip mulch over the surface after planting. This feeds earthworms, moderates soil temperature, and prevents summer cracking. Leave it on the surface rather than digging it in.
Checking progress

After 18 months of consistent amendment, check for earthworms: dig a spade-depth hole (200 mm x 200 mm) and count the earthworms in the removed soil. An unamended UK clay in poor condition has under 10 worms per spade; a well-amended soil after two years should have 25 or more. The presence of earthworms is the most reliable indicator that the soil biology is improving.
What done well looks like

By year two the planting beds are workable two weeks earlier in spring than before amendment. Puddles after rain drain within 12 hours rather than 48. The surface layer crumbles rather than smearing when handled. Earthworm count per spade has risen from 5 to 30 over two seasons.
What done badly looks like

Fresh uncomposted kitchen compost was dug in at high rates and locked up available nitrogen for six weeks during the decomposition process, stunting newly planted seedlings. The soil was double-dug in autumn when waterlogged, smearing the clay structure and making drainage worse for two seasons. Grit was added at 10 kg per square metre without organic matter and made no measurable difference to drainage.
Troubleshooter

attrs.fix: Check for a compaction layer by probing with a metal rod: if resistance increases sharply at 250 to 400 mm, a sub-soil pan is present. Break it up with a sub-soiler fork or hire a rotavator with sub-soil tines. A raised bed structure above the clay may be more practical for planting.
attrs.cause: An impermeable sub-soil pan or hardpan layer below 300 mm is preventing drainage regardless of surface improvement
attrs.symptom: Beds still waterlogged two years after amendment

attrs.fix: Switch to woodier, slower-decomposing mulch materials (wood chip, leaf mould, bark). These resist mineralisation longer and contribute to stable humus over years rather than months. Increase amendment frequency to twice per year.
attrs.cause: Warm, moist clay is highly biologically active and breaks down compost faster than expected; annual additions are being mineralised before they accumulate
attrs.symptom: Compost is disappearing rapidly and organic matter is not building

attrs.fix: Apply a 50 to 75 mm wood chip mulch after every amendment and keep it topped up throughout the growing season. Bare clay without surface protection loses its surface crumb structure within weeks in hot dry weather.
attrs.cause: Soil was left bare without mulch after amendment, allowing summer desiccation and surface capping
attrs.symptom: Surface compacted and cracked in summer despite amendment
